questions.question: Which access specifier allows a member to be accessed by derived classes but not by code outside the class hierarchy?
questions.explanation: The protected access specifier allows members to be accessed by the class itself and by derived classes, but not by code outside the class hierarchy.

questions.question: What happens when a derived class defines a function with the same name and parameters as a base class function?
choices: The base class function is called automatically
The derived class function overrides the base class function
Both functions are called in sequence
questions.explanation: When a derived class defines a function with the same signature as a base class function, the derived class version overrides the base class version. The base class version can still be called using the scope resolution operator.

questions.question: What is a pure virtual function?
choices: A function that cannot be overridden
A virtual function with no body, declared with = 0
A function that is only defined in the derived class
A static function in a base class
questions.explanation: A pure virtual function is declared by assigning 0 to it in the base class (e.g., virtual void draw() = 0;). It has no body in the base class and must be overridden by any concrete derived class.

questions.question: Which statement about abstract classes is true?
choices: You can create objects of an abstract class
An abstract class must contain only pure virtual functions
You cannot create objects of an abstract class, but you can create pointers to one
An abstract class cannot have a constructor
questions.explanation: An abstract class (one with at least one pure virtual function) cannot be instantiated directly, but you can declare pointers and references to it. This enables polymorphism through base class pointers.

questions.question: The ___ keyword is placed before a member function declaration in the base class to allow dynamic binding when called through a base class pointer.
questions.explanation: The virtual keyword enables dynamic (late) binding, so the correct derived class version of the function is called at runtime when accessed through a base class pointer or reference.

questions.question: When a derived class inherits from two or more base classes, this is called ___ inheritance.
questions.explanation: Multiple inheritance occurs when a derived class has two or more base classes. For example: class DerivedClass : public Base1, public Base2 { };
